titleStmt.title: Letter from Michael O'Riordan to Bishop E. T. O'Dwyer, 23 October 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Michael O'Riordan

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p:
This letter was written by Monsignor Michael O'Riordan (1857-1919), rector at the Irish College in Rome, to Bishop Edward Thomas O'Dwyer (1842-1917), who served as the Bishop of Limerick from 1888 until his death.
In this letter O’Riordan discusses a statement which he released. He claims to have written it for the Italian mind and has sent copies to those Bishops mentioned in it. In the letter O'Riordan expresses a desire for the nations to give up fighting.

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
Irish College
Rome Oct 28th 1916 My Lord Bishop Your letter, with enclosed,
has just come. I shall make
the presentation in due time;
and shall convey your message
also. I notice that the sum
is much larger than usual;
which also I shall bring under
his notice. I am glad you liked
my statement. Of course I write
it for the Italian mind; & therefore
I omitted things which would
speak much to persons in
Ireland & in England; but
which would mean nothing to
an Italian, or perhaps make
him misunderstand. I have
sent copies to the Bishops
whom I have mentioned in it;
also to a few who know Italian. It is quite time the
nations gave up fighting. They
would be the laughing-stock
of Europe, if all Europe was
not engaged in the butchery;
— nobody is left out to laugh. Is it not a great lesson in
fractional civilisation? Experimental science has achieved
astonishing results for the benefit
of mankind; then comes the
un-civilised will (without religion)
and uses these results for
man's destruction The late Fr Phelan, the famous
editor of the Western Watchman
of Milwaukee, U.S.A., defined
modern civilisation as " the
improved methods of reciprocal
butchery". He was an exact
thinker. We have just come back
from Tivili.
We begin our Retreat
tomorrow and come ending it
on Nov 1st.. On Nov 1st I
will be here eleven years. How
fast time runs, when duties
multiply! — I am yours obediently M O'Riordan P.S.
I have sent a full
report (un-censored) of your
reply to the Corporation. I asked
if there was such: and I have got
it. Hoping be useful here. I have had some strong letters from
some Cardinals in my Statement
Card. Maffi asked me for more
copies. I am glad I did it. Those
here will have known the facts, before
Card. Gasquet, or Paul de Salis bring fancies.
